[TOOL]: GetInstructions(action="get", explanation="Get assumptions instructions", parameters={"category":"assumptions"}) [TOOL_RESULT]: {"category":"assumptions","content":"# Working with ModelAssumptions\r\n\r\n## Overview\r\n\r\nModelAssumptions are user-input containers that provide values to Variables. They store scalar values that can vary by Scenario and Sensitivity. When a Variable has `defaultFormulaType: Assumptions`, it pulls its values from a ModelAssumption.\r\n\r\n## Key Concepts\r\n\r\n**ModelAssumption**: A container for user-input values with the following key properties:\r\n- **prefix**: The base name before dimensional/time context is added\r\n- **values**: A map of values keyed by Scenario ID\r\n- **sensitivities**: A map of sensitivity overrides keyed by Sensitivity ID\r\n- **sensitivityType**: The type of sensitivity calculation (PercentageChange, Delta, Absolute)\r\n- **unit**: The unit definition including any dimensional modifiers\r\n\r\n**Relationship to Variables**: Variables with `defaultFormulaType: Assumptions` reference a ModelAssumption via `defaultAssumptionId`. The Variable gets its calculated values from the assumption.\r\n\r\n## Values Map\r\n\r\nThe `values` property is a `Dictionary\u003cstring, ModelAssumptionValue\u003e` where:\r\n- **Keys**: Scenario IDs (e.g., `\"-Pt6e56nOUBefHIEvLbi\"`)\r\n- **Values**: ModelAssumptionValue objects\r\n\r\n### ModelAssumptionValue Structure\r\n\r\nEach value entry specifies:\r\n- **modelAssumptionValueType**: Currently only `LocalInput` is supported\r\n- **localInputValue**: The actual value as a string (parsed based on dataType)\r\n\r\n### Example\r\n\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"values\": {\r\n \"-scenarioId1\": {\r\n \"modelAssumptionValueType\": \"LocalInput\",\r\n \"localInputValue\": \"29.99\"\r\n },\r\n \"-scenarioId2\": {\r\n \"modelAssumptionValueType\": \"LocalInput\",\r\n \"localInputValue\": \"34.99\"\r\n }\r\n }\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\n**Important**: If a Scenario is \"based on\" another Scenario and has no explicit value, it inherits from its base Scenario.\r\n\r\n## Sensitivities Map\r\n\r\nThe `sensitivities` property is a `Dictionary\u003cstring, SensitivityValue\u003e` where:\r\n- **Keys**: Sensitivity IDs (e.g., `\"-Pt6e56nOUBefHIEvLbj\"`)\r\n- **Values**: SensitivityValue objects\r\n\r\n### SensitivityValue Structure\r\n\r\nEach sensitivity entry specifies:\r\n- **sensitivityValueType**: Must match the parent assumption's `sensitivityType`\r\n- One of: `percentage`, `delta`, or `absolute` (depending on type)\r\n\r\n### sensitivityType Matching\r\n\r\n**Critical**: The `sensitivityType` on ModelAssumption determines the type for ALL sensitivity values. Individual `SensitivityValue.sensitivityValueType` values must match.\r\n\r\n| sensitivityType | Required Field | Meaning |\r\n|-----------------|---------------|---------|\r\n| PercentageChange | percentage | Multiply base value by (1 + percentage/100) |\r\n| Delta | delta | Add delta to base value |\r\n| Absolute | absolute | Replace base value entirely |\r\n\r\n### Example\r\n\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"sensitivityType\": \"PercentageChange\",\r\n \"sensitivities\": {\r\n \"-sensitivityId1\": {\r\n \"sensitivityValueType\": \"PercentageChange\",\r\n \"percentage\": \"10\"\r\n },\r\n \"-sensitivityId2\": {\r\n \"sensitivityValueType\": \"PercentageChange\",\r\n \"percentage\": \"-5\"\r\n }\r\n }\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\n## Dimensional Assumptions\r\n\r\nAssumptions can represent values across Dimensions (e.g., by Region, by Product). Unlike Variables which expand across Dimensions via `tagParents`, dimensional assumptions require **separate ModelAssumption objects** for each dimension combination.\r\n\r\n### How Dimensions Work\r\n\r\n1. **One Assumption per dimension combination**: For \"Price\" varying by Region (North, South) and Tier (Basic, Premium), you create 4 assumptions\r\n2. **Same prefix, different modifiers**: All share the same `prefix` but have different Unit modifiers\r\n3. **Modifiers on UnitParts**: Dimensional context is stored as `modifiers` on UnitPart objects\r\n\r\n### Example: New Members per Month by Region and Tier\r\n\r\nFor a model with:\r\n- Region Dimension: North, South\r\n- Tier Dimension: Basic, Premium\r\n\r\nYou would create 4 ModelAssumptions, all with `prefix: \"New members per month\"`:\r\n\r\n| Assumption | Value | Unit Modifiers |\r\n|------------|-------|----------------|\r\n| Assumption 1 | 30 | North, Basic |\r\n| Assumption 2 | 10 | North, Premium |\r\n| Assumption 3 | 5 | South, Basic |\r\n| Assumption 4 | 18 | South, Premium |\r\n\r\n### Unit Modifiers Structure\r\n\r\nEach UnitPart can have modifiers that specify dimensional context:\r\n\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"unit\": {\r\n \"unitParts\": [\r\n {\r\n \"tagId\": \"-memberTagId\",\r\n \"modifiers\": [\r\n {\r\n \"tagId\": \"-northTagId\"\r\n },\r\n {\r\n \"tagId\": \"-basicTagId\"\r\n }\r\n ]\r\n }\r\n ]\r\n }\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\nThe `modifiers` array contains references to Concrete Tags that define the dimensional position of this assumption.\r\n\r\n## Time Series Assumptions\r\n\r\nAssumptions can represent values at specific points in time or over specific periods. This is achieved by adding a `dateTimeElement` to a UnitPart.\r\n\r\n### DateTimeElement Types\r\n\r\n| Element Type | Description | Use Case |\r\n|--------------|-------------|----------|\r\n| **Flow** | Period with duration | Flow values (e.g., \"Revenue for Q1 2024\") |\r\n| **Balance** | Point-in-time snapshot | Stock values (e.g., \"Headcount at Dec 2024\") |\r\n| **Timeless** | Period without temporal semantics | Unitless time-varying values (e.g., \"Growth Rate for 2024\") |\r\n\r\n**Important**: All non-constant Assumptions require a `dateTimeElement` matching the UnitPart's TimeType. Constant Assumptions (where the Variable has `isConstant: true`) have no `dateTimeElement` — a single value covers all periods.\r\n\r\n### DateTimeSeriesDate Formats\r\n\r\nThe `dateString` format depends on the `dateType`:\r\n\r\n| dateType | Format | Example |\r\n|----------|--------|---------|\r\n| Day | `yyyyMMdd` | `\"20240115\"` (January 15, 2024) |\r\n| Week | `yyyyMMdd` | `\"20240121\"` (end of week, e.g., Sunday) |\r\n| Month | `yyyyMM` | `\"202401\"` (January 2024) |\r\n| Quarter | `yyyyQ[1-4]` | `\"2024Q1\"` (Q1 2024) |\r\n| Year | `yyyy` | `\"2024\"` |\r\n\r\n**Note**: Quarter and Year formats use calendar year conventions (Q1 = Jan-Mar, year ends December 31). The system will interpret these correctly based on the model's fiscal year settings.\r\n\r\n### Month-Specific Options\r\n\r\nWhen `dateType` is `Month`, you can specify when in the month:\r\n\r\n| monthDateType | Description |\r\n|---------------|-------------|\r\n| EndOfMonth | End of the specified month (default) |\r\n| DayInMonth | Specific day, requires `dayOfMonth` (1-28) |\r\n\r\n### DateTimeElement Structure\r\n\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"dateTimeElement\": {\r\n \"elementType\": \"Flow\",\r\n \"dateType\": \"Quarter\",\r\n \"dateString\": \"2024Q1\",\r\n \"duration\": 1\r\n }\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\nFor Balance (snapshot) elements, omit `duration`:\r\n\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"dateTimeElement\": {\r\n \"elementType\": \"Balance\",\r\n \"dateType\": \"Month\",\r\n \"dateString\": \"202412\",\r\n \"monthDateType\": \"EndOfMonth\"\r\n }\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\n### Pattern: Time Series Assumption\r\n\r\nA quarterly revenue assumption for Q1 2024:\r\n\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"action\": \"create\",\r\n \"object_type\": \"ModelAssumption\",\r\n \"parameters\": {\r\n \"prefix\": \"Revenue\",\r\n \"dataType\": \"Double\",\r\n \"values\": {\r\n \"-baseScenarioId\": {\r\n \"modelAssumptionValueType\": \"LocalInput\",\r\n \"localInputValue\": \"1000000\"\r\n }\r\n },\r\n \"unit\": {\r\n \"unitParts\": [\r\n {\r\n \"tagId\": \"-revenueTagId\",\r\n \"timeType\": \"Flow\",\r\n \"dateTimeElement\": {\r\n \"elementType\": \"Flow\",\r\n \"dateType\": \"Quarter\",\r\n \"dateString\": \"2024Q1\",\r\n \"duration\": 1\r\n }\r\n }\r\n ]\r\n }\r\n }\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\n### Pattern: Balance Snapshot Assumption\r\n\r\nA monthly balance (e.g., headcount at end of month):\r\n\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"action\": \"create\",\r\n \"object_type\": \"ModelAssumption\",\r\n \"parameters\": {\r\n \"prefix\": \"Headcount\",\r\n \"dataType\": \"Integer\",\r\n \"values\": {\r\n \"-baseScenarioId\": {\r\n \"modelAssumptionValueType\": \"LocalInput\",\r\n \"localInputValue\": \"150\"\r\n }\r\n },\r\n \"unit\": {\r\n \"unitParts\": [\r\n {\r\n \"tagId\": \"-headcountTagId\",\r\n \"timeType\": \"Balance\",\r\n \"dateTimeElement\": {\r\n \"elementType\": \"Balance\",\r\n \"dateType\": \"Month\",\r\n \"dateString\": \"202412\"\r\n }\r\n }\r\n ]\r\n }\r\n }\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\n### Pattern: Unitless Time Series\r\n\r\nFor a unitless value at a specific time (no tag, just time context):\r\n\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"action\": \"create\",\r\n \"object_type\": \"ModelAssumption\",\r\n \"parameters\": {\r\n \"prefix\": \"Growth Rate\",\r\n \"dataType\": \"Double\",\r\n \"values\": {\r\n \"-baseScenarioId\": {\r\n \"modelAssumptionValueType\": \"LocalInput\",\r\n \"localInputValue\": \"0.05\"\r\n }\r\n },\r\n \"unit\": {\r\n \"unitParts\": [\r\n {\r\n \"tagId\": null,\r\n \"timeType\": \"Timeless\",\r\n \"dateTimeElement\": {\r\n \"elementType\": \"Timeless\",\r\n \"dateType\": \"Year\",\r\n \"dateString\": \"2024\"\r\n }\r\n }\r\n ]\r\n }\r\n }\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\n### Time Series Pitfalls\r\n\r\n**Pitfall 1: Missing duration for Flow**\r\n\r\nFlow elements require a `duration` (minimum 1). Balance elements should not have duration.\r\n\r\n**Pitfall 2: Mismatched timeType and elementType**\r\n\r\nThe `timeType` on UnitPart should match the `elementType` in dateTimeElement:\r\n- `timeType: \"Flow\"` → `elementType: \"Flow\"`\r\n- `timeType: \"Balance\"` → `elementType: \"Balance\"`\r\n- `timeType: \"Timeless\"` → `elementType: \"Timeless\"`\r\n\r\n**Pitfall 3: Wrong date format**\r\n\r\nEach dateType has a specific format. Using `\"2024-01\"` instead of `\"202401\"` for Month will fail validation.\r\n\r\n## Common Patterns\r\n\r\n### Pattern 1: Simple Scalar Assumption\r\n\r\nA single value that doesn't vary by Scenario:\r\n\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"action\": \"create\",\r\n \"object_type\": \"ModelAssumption\",\r\n \"parameters\": {\r\n \"prefix\": \"Tax Rate\",\r\n \"dataType\": \"Double\",\r\n \"values\": {\r\n \"-baseScenarioId\": {\r\n \"modelAssumptionValueType\": \"LocalInput\",\r\n \"localInputValue\": \"0.25\"\r\n }\r\n }\r\n }\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\n### Pattern 2: Multi-Scenario Assumption\r\n\r\nValues that differ by Scenario:\r\n\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"action\": \"create\",\r\n \"object_type\": \"ModelAssumption\",\r\n \"parameters\": {\r\n \"prefix\": \"Revenue Growth Rate\",\r\n \"dataType\": \"Double\",\r\n \"values\": {\r\n \"-baseScenarioId\": {\r\n \"modelAssumptionValueType\": \"LocalInput\",\r\n \"localInputValue\": \"0.05\"\r\n },\r\n \"-optimisticScenarioId\": {\r\n \"modelAssumptionValueType\": \"LocalInput\",\r\n \"localInputValue\": \"0.08\"\r\n },\r\n \"-pessimisticScenarioId\": {\r\n \"modelAssumptionValueType\": \"LocalInput\",\r\n \"localInputValue\": \"0.02\"\r\n }\r\n }\r\n }\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\n### Pattern 3: Assumption with Sensitivities\r\n\r\nAdd sensitivity analysis capability:\r\n\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"action\": \"create\",\r\n \"object_type\": \"ModelAssumption\",\r\n \"parameters\": {\r\n \"prefix\": \"Discount Rate\",\r\n \"dataType\": \"Double\",\r\n \"sensitivityType\": \"Delta\",\r\n \"values\": {\r\n \"-baseScenarioId\": {\r\n \"modelAssumptionValueType\": \"LocalInput\",\r\n \"localInputValue\": \"0.08\"\r\n }\r\n },\r\n \"sensitivities\": {\r\n \"-highSensitivityId\": {\r\n \"sensitivityValueType\": \"Delta\",\r\n \"delta\": \"0.02\"\r\n },\r\n \"-lowSensitivityId\": {\r\n \"sensitivityValueType\": \"Delta\",\r\n \"delta\": \"-0.02\"\r\n }\r\n }\r\n }\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\n## Common Pitfalls\r\n\r\n### Pitfall 1: Confusing Values Keys with Sensitivities Keys\r\n\r\n**Mistake**: Using the wrong ID type as map keys.\r\n\r\n**Reality**:\r\n- `values` keys are **Scenario IDs**\r\n- `sensitivities` keys are **Sensitivity IDs**\r\n\r\nThese are different object types with different IDs.\r\n\r\n### Pitfall 2: Mismatching sensitivityType\r\n\r\n**Mistake**: Setting `sensitivityType: PercentageChange` but providing `delta` values in sensitivities.\r\n\r\n**Reality**: The `sensitivityValueType` on each SensitivityValue must match the parent assumption's `sensitivityType`.\r\n\r\n### Pitfall 3: Expecting Dimensional Expansion\r\n\r\n**Mistake**: Thinking one assumption can cover multiple dimension combinations.\r\n\r\n**Reality**: Each dimension combination requires its own ModelAssumption. Use Unit modifiers to differentiate them.\r\n\r\n### Pitfall 4: Numeric Values as Numbers\r\n\r\n**Mistake**: Providing numeric values as JSON numbers (`\"localInputValue\": 29.99`).\r\n\r\n**Reality**: All values are stored as strings. Always use string format (`\"localInputValue\": \"29.99\"`).\r\n\r\n## Best Practices\r\n\r\n1. **Use descriptive prefixes** that clearly identify what the assumption represents\r\n2. **Provide values for the Base Scenario** at minimum - other scenarios can inherit\r\n3. **Match sensitivityType with your analysis needs**:\r\n - Use `PercentageChange` for multiplicative sensitivity (e.g., revenue changes)\r\n - Use `Delta` for additive sensitivity (e.g., rate adjustments)\r\n - Use `Absolute` for replacement values\r\n4. **Create dimensional assumptions systematically** - ensure all combinations are covered\r\n5. **Query existing Scenarios and Sensitivities** before creating assumptions to get valid IDs\r\n","description":"Object reference: ModelAssumption properties, values maps, sensitivities, dimensional assumptions, unit modifiers","title":"Working with ModelAssumptions","topics":["ModelAssumption","Scenarios","Sensitivities","Dimensions","UnitModifiers","values"]} [ASSISTANT]: Now I understand the approach. At Level 1, I need to use the inline assumption grid on TimeSegments. Let me check how that works specifically. [TOOL]: GetInstructions(action="get", explanation="Get variables instructions", parameters={"category":"variables"}) [TOOL_RESULT]: {"category":"variables","content":"# Working with Variables\r\n\r\n## Overview\r\n\r\nVariables are the primary calculation containers in Models. This guide covers complex Variable properties and patterns that require deeper understanding beyond basic schema descriptions.\r\n\r\n## Targeted Editing (Partial Updates)\r\n\r\nVariable updates support targeted edits. Only include the properties you want to change. Omitted properties are preserved.\r\n\r\n- **Omitted property**: keeps the existing value\r\n- **Explicit null**: clears the value (when allowed by the schema)\r\n\r\nExample (update just the name):\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"id\": \"var-123\",\r\n \"name\": \"Updated Variable Name\"\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\n## Key Concepts\r\n\r\n**Variable**: A calculation container with a `defaultFormulaType` that determines its behavior:\r\n- **Calculation**: Uses mathematical formulas referencing other Variables\r\n- **Assumption**: Gets values from ModelAssumption objects (user inputs)\r\n- **Aggregation**: Collects Variables representing Dimension parts into a Dimension-level Variable\r\n\r\n**Dimensions**: Abstract Tags with Concrete Tag children that expand Variables across multiple categories. When a Variable references an Abstract Tag via `tagParents`, it automatically expands to generate separate calculation lines for each Concrete child.\r\n\r\n## Defining Calculations with formulaText\r\n\r\n### Overview\r\n\r\nWhen creating or updating Calculation Variables, you can define the formula using `formulaText` - a human-readable string format - instead of the verbose `calculationParts` array.\r\n\r\n### Where to Use\r\n\r\nThe `formulaText` property can be used inside any `calculation` or `defaultCalculation` object:\r\n\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"name\": \"Profit\",\r\n \"defaultFormulaType\": \"Calculation\",\r\n \"defaultCalculation\": {\r\n \"formulaText\": \"Revenue - Costs\"\r\n }\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\n### Syntax\r\n\r\n#### Variable References\r\n- Use the Variable name directly: `Revenue`\r\n- Quote names with special characters: `\"Revenue (Net)\"`\r\n- Quote names with spaces: `\"Total Revenue\"`\r\n\r\n#### Operators\r\n- Addition: `+`\r\n- Subtraction: `-`\r\n- Multiplication: `*`\r\n- Division: `/`\r\n- Exponentiation: `^`\r\n- Parentheses for grouping: `(Revenue - Costs) * 100`\r\n\r\n#### Constants\r\n- Numbers: `100`, `3.14`, `-5`\r\n- Negative values: `-100` or `0 - 100`\r\n\r\n#### Period Offsets\r\n- Previous period: `Revenue[-1]`\r\n- Two periods back: `Revenue[-2]`\r\n- Useful for growth calculations: `Revenue - Revenue[-1]`\r\n\r\n#### Period References\r\nThe `Periods` keyword returns the current period's end date as a date value. This is useful for creating timing flexibility, e.g. `Is Live = Periods \u003e Launch Date`\r\n\r\nKey points:\r\n- Returns the **end date** of the period (e.g., 31 Dec for a Calendar Year)\r\n- Supports offsets: `Periods[-1]` returns the previous period's end date\r\n- Compare against date-type Assumption Variables (Variables with date values)\r\n\r\nFinancial modeling best practice with Periods:\r\n- When flexible timing is required use Variables with DateType `Date` (Model periods don't have a time component)\r\n- It is always best to create a `Boolean` Variable for a timing condition (e.g. `Service is live`) and then to multiply that by the relevant Variable, rather than perform both timing logic and calculation in a single Variable\r\n\r\n#### Functions\r\n- Standard functions: `SUM(...)`, `MAX(...)`, `MIN(...)`, `IF(...)`\r\n- Example: `IF(Revenue \u003e 0, Profit / Revenue, 0)`\r\n\r\n### Examples\r\n\r\n**Simple calculation:**\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"defaultCalculation\": {\r\n \"formulaText\": \"Revenue - Costs\"\r\n }\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\n**With period offset (growth calculation):**\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"defaultCalculation\": {\r\n \"formulaText\": \"Revenue - Revenue[-1]\"\r\n }\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\n**With parentheses and constants:**\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"defaultCalculation\": {\r\n \"formulaText\": \"(Revenue - Costs) / Revenue * 100\"\r\n }\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\n**With quoted variable names:**\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"defaultCalculation\": {\r\n \"formulaText\": \"\\\"Net Revenue\\\" - \\\"Operating Costs\\\"\"\r\n }\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\n**With period reference (date comparison):**\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"defaultCalculation\": {\r\n \"formulaText\": \"Periods \u003e \\\"Launch Date\\\"\"\r\n }\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\n### Precedence Rules\r\n\r\n- If both `formulaText` and `calculationParts` are provided, `calculationParts` takes precedence\r\n- `formulaText` is converted to `calculationParts` before the Variable is created/updated\r\n- The API response includes `formulaText` generated from the stored `calculationParts`\r\n\r\n### Error Handling\r\n\r\nParse errors return structured error messages with:\r\n- Position in the formula where the error occurred\r\n- What was found vs. what was expected\r\n- Hints for resolution (e.g., \"Variable 'Unknown' not found in Model\")\r\n\r\n## Aggregation Variables\r\n\r\n### Critical Understanding: Aggregation is NOT Summing\r\n\r\n**Common Misconception**: Aggregation Variables \"sum\" other Variables.\r\n\r\n**Reality**: Aggregation Variables **collect** Variables that each represent a part of a Dimension into a Variable of that Dimension.\r\n\r\n### Example: Regional Revenues\r\n\r\nSuppose you have:\r\n- **Revenues-South**: Calculation Variable for southern region revenues\r\n- **Revenues-North**: Calculation Variable for northern region revenues\r\n- Both are Concrete Tags under the \"Region\" Abstract Tag (Dimension)\r\n\r\nYou can create:\r\n- **Revenues by Region**: Aggregation Variable that collects Revenues-South and Revenues-North\r\n\r\nThe Aggregation Variable expands to show:\r\n- Revenues by Region (South) = Revenues-South\r\n- Revenues by Region (North) = Revenues-North\r\n\r\nEach region may calculate revenues differently, but the Aggregation Variable provides a unified Dimension-level view.\r\n\r\n### When to Use Aggregation\r\n\r\n- When you have Variables representing Dimension parts (e.g., regions, products, markets)\r\n- When you want a Dimension-level Variable that combines these parts\r\n- When each part may have different calculation logic\r\n\r\n### Property: `aggregatedVariableIds`\r\n\r\n- **Type**: `array[string]` - Array of Variable IDs\r\n- **Relevant When**: `defaultFormulaType` is `Aggregation`\r\n- **Purpose**: Lists the Variables representing Dimension parts to collect\r\n- **Note**: These Variables must represent parts of the same Dimension(s) that the Aggregation Variable uses\r\n\r\n## contextVarianceMap\r\n\r\n### Purpose\r\n\r\nThe `contextVarianceMap` defines which unit parts vary with which Dimensions. This is critical for Variables with both:\r\n- **Dimensions** (via `tagParents`)\r\n- **Defined units** (via `unitOption: Defined`)\r\n\r\n### When It's Relevant\r\n\r\n- Variable has `unitOption: Defined` (not Inferred)\r\n- Variable has Dimensions (via `tagParents` with at least one parent)\r\n- **Both conditions must be true**\r\n\r\n### Structure\r\n\r\nThe map is a dictionary where:\r\n- **Key**: Unit part ID (e.g., \"GBP\", \"Member\", \"Month\")\r\n- **Value**: Set of Dimension (TagParent) IDs that this unit part varies with\r\n\r\n### Example\r\n\r\nSuppose a Variable has:\r\n- Unit: \"GBP per Member per Month\" (with UnitPart IDs: `up-gbp-123`, `up-member-456`, `up-month-789`)\r\n- Dimensions: \"Region\" (Tag ID: `tag-region-abc`) and \"Product\" (Tag ID: `tag-product-def`)\r\n\r\nThe default `contextVarianceMap` would be:\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"up-gbp-123\": [\"tag-region-abc\", \"tag-product-def\"],\r\n \"up-member-456\": [\"tag-region-abc\", \"tag-product-def\"],\r\n \"up-month-789\": [\"tag-region-abc\", \"tag-product-def\"]\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\n**Important**: The map uses actual object IDs, not human-readable names:\r\n- **Keys**: UnitPart IDs from the Variable's Unit (use `get-details` on the Unit to find these)\r\n- **Values**: Tag IDs of the Dimensions from `tagParents` (Abstract Tag IDs)\r\n\r\nThis means all unit parts vary with all Dimensions (the default).\r\n\r\n### Custom contextVarianceMap\r\n\r\nYou only need to provide a custom `contextVarianceMap` when:\r\n- You want specific unit parts to vary with specific Dimensions only\r\n- The default \"all vary with all\" doesn't match your requirements\r\n\r\n**Important**: Each Dimension must vary with at least one unit part. Otherwise, there wouldn't be variance for that Dimension.\r\n\r\n### Property: `contextVarianceMap`\r\n\r\n- **Type**: `object` (dictionary mapping unit part IDs to Dimension ID sets)\r\n- **Relevant When**: `unitOption` is `Defined` AND Variable has Dimensions\r\n- **Default**: Automatically generated as \"all Dimensions vary with all unit parts\"\r\n- **Rarely Required**: Most Variables use the default map\r\n\r\n## groupSummaryType\r\n\r\n### Purpose\r\n\r\nControls how a Variable summarizes across all Dimensions when creating summary lines.\r\n\r\n### Values\r\n\r\n#### `None`\r\n- No automatic summary behavior\r\n- Variable appears only at the detailed Dimension level\r\n\r\n#### `Sum`\r\n- Always performs a simple sum across all Dimension items\r\n- Example: If you have Revenues by Region (South, North), Sum gives Total Revenues = South + North\r\n\r\n#### `Auto`\r\n- Performs the Variable's calculation on the summary lines of referenced Variables\r\n- Useful when the result differs from a simple sum\r\n- **Example**: Weighted average price\r\n - If Price = Revenue / Volume\r\n - Auto calculates: Total Price = Total Revenue / Total Volume\r\n - This is different from averaging individual prices\r\n\r\n#### `Rearranged`\r\n- Similar to Auto, but requires a parent Variable reference\r\n- Used when the summary calculation can only be computed from parent Variables\r\n- **Example**: Average price when Price is an input (not calculated)\r\n - If Price is an input Variable (not Revenue/Volume)\r\n - You need to rearrange: Price = Revenue / Volume\r\n - But Revenue and Volume are parent Variables\r\n - Rearranged uses `groupSummaryRearrangedParentId` to reference the parent for calculation\r\n\r\n### When to Use Each\r\n\r\n| Use Case | groupSummaryType | Notes |\r\n|----------|------------------|-------|\r\n| Simple totals | `Sum` | Direct sum of all Dimension items |\r\n| Calculated metrics | `Auto` | Recalculate formula on summary lines |\r\n| Input-based metrics | `Rearranged` | Calculate from parent Variables |\r\n\r\n### Property: `groupSummaryType`\r\n\r\n- **Type**: `enum` - `None`, `Sum`, `Auto`, `Rearranged`\r\n- **Default**: `None`\r\n- **Purpose**: Controls Dimension summary behavior\r\n\r\n### Property: `groupSummaryRearrangedParentId`\r\n\r\n- **Type**: `string` - Variable ID\r\n- **Required When**: `groupSummaryType` is `Rearranged`\r\n- **Purpose**: References the parent Variable to base the Rearranged summary calculation on\r\n- **Example**: For weighted averages requiring parent data\r\n\r\n## Unit System\r\n\r\n### Defined vs Inferred\r\n\r\n#### `unitOption: Defined`\r\n- Unit is explicitly set via the `unit` property\r\n- Used for Assumption and Aggregation Variables (default)\r\n- Can also be used for Calculation Variables\r\n\r\n#### `unitOption: Inferred`\r\n- Unit is automatically calculated from the formula\r\n- Only available for Calculation Variables\r\n- Based on the Variables referenced in the calculation\r\n- If unit cannot be calculated, a unit error is shown\r\n\r\n### Unit Factoring (`applyFactoring`)\r\n\r\n**Purpose**: Automatically adjusts calculations when the Variable's unit factor differs from the calculated unit factor.\r\n\r\n**How It Works**:\r\n- Factoring is done automatically based on units\r\n- Only works if `unitOption` is `Defined`\r\n- The Variable's unit must have a different factor from the calculated unit\r\n- Example: Variable has unit in millions (factorPower 6/1), but calculation returns thousands (factorPower 3/1)\r\n- Setting `applyFactoring: true` automatically adjusts the calculation (divides by 10^3)\r\n\r\n**Property**: `applyFactoring`\r\n- **Type**: `boolean`\r\n- **Default**: `false`\r\n- **Purpose**: Auto-adjust when Variable unit factor differs from calculated unit factor\r\n- **Note**: User doesn't need to multiply or divide within the Variable's calculation\r\n\r\n### Unit Validation\r\n\r\n- Automatic unit compatibility checking across Variable references\r\n- Unit inconsistencies generate warnings, not errors - calculations still execute\r\n- `overrideUnitError: true` allows bypassing unit validation when needed\r\n\r\n**When to use `overrideUnitError`:**\r\n- The calculated unit doesn't match the Defined unit, but the result is semantically correct\r\n- Example: `Balance[-1] × Timeless` infers as `Balance[-1]`, but you know it should be `Flow`\r\n- Always set `overrideUnitError: true` at the same time as setting the Defined unit — don't set the Defined unit first and then override in a separate step\r\n\r\n**When to use a compound unit instead:**\r\n- When a rate converts between TimeTypes (e.g., stock to flow), give it a compound unit\r\n- Example: Churn Rate = `Member [Flow] / Member [Balance[-1]]` — then `Members[-1] × Churn Rate` resolves to `Member [Flow]` without needing an override\r\n- Compound units are preferred over overrides because they make the algebra self-documenting\r\n\r\n## NumberFormat Selection\r\n\r\nThe `numberFormatId` property controls how Variable values are displayed. Always set an appropriate format.\r\n\r\n### Common Inbuilt Format IDs\r\n\r\n| ID | Format | Use For |\r\n|----|--------|---------|\r\n| `General` | General | Boolean, Validation, String |\r\n| `ThousandSeparator0Dp` | #,##0 | Standard integers (default) |\r\n| `TwoDecimalPlaces` | 0.00 | Decimals without thousands separator |\r\n| `Pct1Dp` | 0.0% | Percentages (growth, rate, margin, yield) |\r\n| `Date1` | dd mmm yy | Date values |\r\n| `GBP2Dp` | £#,##0.00 | GBP currency |\r\n| `USD2Dp` | $#,##0.00 | USD currency |\r\n| `EUR2Dp` | €#,##0.00 | EUR currency |\r\n\r\nUse `Pct1Dp` when Variable name contains: %, pct, percentage, growth, rate, inflation, margin, yield.\r\n\r\nIf the required format isn't listed above, use `filter` to find other available formats, or `create` to add a new one.\r\n\r\n## TimeSegments\r\n\r\n### Purpose\r\n\r\nAllow Variables to change formula behavior across different time periods.\r\n\r\n### Types of TimeSegments\r\n\r\n#### Assumption TimeSegments\r\n- Provide values from ModelAssumption objects for specific periods/dimensions\r\n- Can override the default assumption for specific time ranges\r\n\r\n#### Calculation TimeSegments\r\n- Provide different calculation formulas for specific time periods\r\n- **Critical Constraint**: Calculation formulas within TimeSegments can only reference the same Variable with period offsets (e.g., `[-1]` for previous period)\r\n- They cannot reference other Variables to prevent circular dependencies\r\n\r\n### Creating Assumptions Inline with TimeSegments\r\n\r\nWhen creating a Variable with Assumption TimeSegments, you can provide assumption values directly as a 2D grid. This eliminates the need to create ModelAssumption objects separately.\r\n\r\n#### Grid Convention\r\n\r\n- **Columns**: Time periods (chronological order, determined by TimeSegment's date range)\r\n- **Rows**: Dimension cross-product (if Variable has dimensions via `tagParents`)\r\n- **Empty/null values**: Skip (no assumption created for that cell)\r\n\r\n#### Example: Simple Time Series (No Dimensions)\r\n\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"name\": \"Growth Rate\",\r\n \"defaultFormulaType\": \"Assumptions\",\r\n \"timeSegments\": [{\r\n \"start\": { \"segmentPeriodType\": \"Auto\" },\r\n \"duration\": { \"segmentDurationType\": \"PeriodsGeneric\", \"genericPeriods\": 6 },\r\n \"assumptions\": {\r\n \"values\": [\r\n [\"0.05\", \"0.06\", \"0.07\", \"0.08\", \"0.09\", \"0.10\"]\r\n ]\r\n }\r\n }]\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\nThe Variable's name (\"Growth Rate\") is automatically used as the assumption prefix.\r\n\r\n#### Example: Dimensional Variable (Region Dimension)\r\n\r\nFor a Variable with Region dimension (North, South) and 3 time periods:\r\n\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"name\": \"Sales Growth\",\r\n \"defaultFormulaType\": \"Assumptions\",\r\n \"tagParents\": { \"parents\": [{ \"tagId\": \"-regionDimensionId\" }] },\r\n \"timeSegments\": [{\r\n \"start\": { \"segmentPeriodType\": \"Auto\" },\r\n \"duration\": { \"segmentDurationType\": \"PeriodsGeneric\", \"genericPeriods\": 3 },\r\n \"assumptions\": {\r\n \"values\": [\r\n [\"0.05\", \"0.06\", \"0.07\"],\r\n [\"0.04\", \"0.05\", \"0.06\"]\r\n ]\r\n }\r\n }]\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\nRow order is deterministic (ordered by TagGroupWithParents.OrderedId). Use `expand-dimensions` action to discover the exact grid shape.\r\n\r\n#### Grid Shape Discovery\r\n\r\nBefore providing values, you can use the `expand-dimensions` action to discover the expected grid shape:\r\n\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"objectType\": \"Variable\",\r\n \"action\": \"expand-dimensions\",\r\n \"id\": \"var-123\",\r\n \"timeSegmentIndex\": 0\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\nReturns:\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"columns\": [\"2021\", \"2022\", \"2023\"],\r\n \"rows\": [\"North\", \"South\"],\r\n \"shape\": [2, 3]\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\nThis tells you:\r\n- The Variable expects a 2×3 grid\r\n- Rows are \"North\" then \"South\" (dimension cross-product)\r\n- Columns are \"2021\", \"2022\", \"2023\" (time periods)\r\n\r\n#### How It Works\r\n\r\nWhen you provide an `assumptions` object on a TimeSegment:\r\n1. The TimeSegment's formula type is automatically set to `Assumptions`\r\n2. After the Variable is created, ModelAssumption objects are generated from the grid\r\n3. Each grid cell creates one assumption with the correct Unit (including time and dimension modifiers)\r\n4. Assumptions are linked to the TimeSegment's formula\r\n5. Values use the Base Scenario by default\r\n\r\n#### When to Use\r\n\r\n- **Creating new Variables with Assumption TimeSegments**: Simplest approach\r\n- **Simple time series**: Just need to provide values across periods\r\n- **Dimensional assumptions**: Values vary by dimension and time\r\n\r\n#### When NOT to Use\r\n\r\n- **Updating existing assumptions**: Use direct ModelAssumption updates instead\r\n- **Multi-scenario values**: Use standard ModelAssumption creation with explicit values per scenario\r\n- **Complex sensitivity setups**: Create ModelAssumptions directly with sensitivity configuration\r\n\r\n### defaultAssumptionId\r\n\r\n**Purpose**: Fallback assumption for any period/dimensions in a TimeSegment that doesn't have a matching assumption.\r\n\r\n**When Used**:\r\n- Very rarely in practice\r\n- Typically, all assumption IDs are provided within TimeSegments\r\n- Example: If you want the Variable to have the value 0 except for periods that you provide a value for\r\n\r\n**Property**: `defaultAssumptionId`\r\n- **Type**: `string` - ModelAssumption ID\r\n- **Available For**: All formula types (even Calculation Variables can have Assumption TimeSegments)\r\n- **Purpose**: Fallback assumption for periods/dimensions without matching TimeSegment assumption\r\n\r\n### TimeSegment Coverage\r\n\r\nTimeSegments must collectively cover all periods without gaps or overlaps.\r\n\r\n## periodType\r\n\r\n### Purpose\r\n\r\nOverrides the Model's default period for this specific Variable.\r\n\r\n### Rules\r\n\r\n- Must be set to a broader time interval than the Model timings\r\n- Example: If Model is in weeks, you can define a Variable in months\r\n- `None` means \"use the Model's default period\"\r\n\r\n### Property: `periodType`\r\n\r\n- **Type**: `enum` - `None`, `Day`, `Week`, `Month`, `Quarter`, `YearCalendar`, `YearFiscal`\r\n- **Default**: `None` (use Model default)\r\n- **Purpose**: Override Model's default period for this Variable\r\n\r\n## validationDimensionId\r\n\r\n### Purpose\r\n\r\nDefines dropdown options for Validation-type Variables.\r\n\r\n### How It Works\r\n\r\n- Variable must have `dataType: Validation`\r\n- `validationDimensionId` references an Abstract Tag (Dimension)\r\n- The Concrete Tag children of that Abstract Tag become the dropdown options\r\n- Example: \"Yes/No\" Dimension with Concrete children \"Yes\" and \"No\"\r\n\r\n### Property: `validationDimensionId`\r\n\r\n- **Type**: `string` - Abstract Tag ID\r\n- **Required When**: `dataType` is `Validation`\r\n- **Purpose**: Abstract Tag defining dropdown options for Validation-type Variables\r\n\r\n## Common Patterns and Pitfalls\r\n\r\n### Pattern 1: Dimensional Aggregation\r\n\r\n**Scenario**: You have revenue calculations for each region, and want a total revenue Variable.\r\n\r\n**Correct Approach**:\r\n1. Create Calculation Variables: \"Revenues-South\", \"Revenues-North\" (each with Region Dimension)\r\n2. Create Aggregation Variable: \"Total Revenues\" with `aggregatedVariableIds: [\"Revenues-South\", \"Revenues-North\"]`\r\n3. Set `groupSummaryType: Sum` if you want summary lines\r\n\r\n**Incorrect**: Trying to sum the Variables in a Calculation formula - use Aggregation instead.\r\n\r\n### Pattern 2: Unit Factoring\r\n\r\n**Scenario**: Your Model works in thousands, but you want to display a Variable in millions.\r\n\r\n**Correct Approach**:\r\n1. Set Variable unit to millions (factorPower 6/1)\r\n2. Ensure calculation returns thousands (factorPower 3/1)\r\n3. Set `applyFactoring: true`\r\n4. System automatically adjusts (divides by 10^3)\r\n\r\n**Incorrect**: Manually dividing in the calculation formula.\r\n\r\n### Pattern 3: Weighted Averages\r\n\r\n**Scenario**: You want average price across all products.\r\n\r\n**If Price is Calculated** (Price = Revenue / Volume):\r\n- Use `groupSummaryType: Auto`\r\n- System recalculates: Total Price = Total Revenue / Total Volume\r\n\r\n**If Price is an Input**:\r\n- Use `groupSummaryType: Rearranged`\r\n- Set `groupSummaryRearrangedParentId` to reference Revenue or Volume parent\r\n- System rearranges calculation from parent Variables\r\n\r\n### Pitfall 1: Confusing Aggregation with Summing\r\n\r\n**Mistake**: Thinking `aggregatedVariableIds` creates a sum.\r\n\r\n**Reality**: Aggregation collects Dimension parts, not sums values.\r\n\r\n### Pitfall 2: Missing contextVarianceMap Condition\r\n\r\n**Mistake**: Providing `contextVarianceMap` when Variable has no Dimensions.\r\n\r\n**Reality**: `contextVarianceMap` is only relevant when Variable has both Dimensions AND Defined unit.\r\n\r\n### Pitfall 3: Wrong groupSummaryType\r\n\r\n**Mistake**: Using `Sum` for calculated metrics that need recalculation.\r\n\r\n**Reality**: Use `Auto` or `Rearranged` when the summary result differs from a simple sum.\r\n\r\n### Pitfall 4: Self-Referencing Variable with Inferred Unit\r\n\r\n**Mistake**: Creating a Calculation Variable that references itself with a period offset (e.g., `Balance[-1] + Inflow`) and leaving `unitOption: Inferred`.\r\n\r\n**Reality**: Self-referencing Variables require `unitOption: Defined`. The Unit system cannot resolve self-references to determine the inferred type. Always define the Unit explicitly with the appropriate TimeType (typically Balance for accumulator patterns).\r\n\r\n## Best Practices\r\n\r\n1. **Use Aggregation for Dimension-based collection**, not for simple summing\r\n2. **Rely on default contextVarianceMap** unless you have specific requirements\r\n3. **Use Auto for calculated metrics** that need recalculation on summary lines\r\n4. **Use Rearranged only when necessary** - when calculation requires parent Variables\r\n5. **Set applyFactoring when unit factors differ** - don't manually adjust in formulas\r\n6. **Use defaultAssumptionId sparingly** - prefer explicit TimeSegment assumptions\r\n7. **Validate periodType compatibility** - must be broader than Model timings\r\n\r\n## Retrieving Calculated Values\r\n\r\n### get-values Operation\r\n\r\nRetrieve calculated values for one or more Variables:\r\n\r\n```\r\nQueryObject(object_type='Variable', action='get-values', parameters={\r\n 'ids': ['var-1', 'var-2'],\r\n 'periods': 'sample', # 'all', 'first', 'last', 'first-N', 'last-N', 'sample', or specific periods\r\n 'dimensions': 'summary', # 'summary', 'all', or specific paths like 'UK/Small'\r\n 'case': 'Base/Base', # Scenario/Sensitivity\r\n 'includeAnalysis': true # Adds trend, min/max, growth rate\r\n})\r\n```\r\n\r\n**Key considerations**:\r\n- Use `periods: 'sample'` for a quick snapshot (first, middle, last)\r\n- Use `dimensions: 'summary'` unless you need individual dimension values\r\n- The `includeAnalysis` option adds trend classification and growth rate\r\n\r\n### compare-cases Operation\r\n\r\nCompare values between two cases (Scenario/Sensitivity combinations):\r\n\r\n```\r\nQueryObject(object_type='Variable', action='compare-cases', parameters={\r\n 'ids': ['profit'],\r\n 'baseCase': 'Base/Base', # Required: baseline for comparison\r\n 'compareCase': 'Lean/Base', # Required: case to compare against baseline\r\n 'periods': 'last'\r\n})\r\n```\r\n\r\nReturns values with variance from baseline (absolute and percentage difference).\r\n\r\n### trace-dependencies with Values\r\n\r\nAdd `includeValues: true` to see values alongside the dependency tree:\r\n\r\n```\r\nModelAction(action='trace-dependencies', variableId='profit', includeValues=true, valuePeriod='last')\r\n```\r\n\r\n### valueSummary in Filter\r\n\r\nAdd `valueSummary` to filter properties to see first/last values and trend:\r\n\r\n```\r\nQueryObject(object_type='Variable', action='filter', parameters={\r\n 'properties': ['name', 'valueSummary']\r\n})\r\n```\r\n\r\nReturns `valueSummary: { first: { period, value }, last: { period, value }, trend: 'growing' }` for each Variable.\r\n","description":"Object reference: Variable properties, formulaText, CalculationParts, TimeSegments, DimensionReferences, Aggregation, Units, retrieving calculated values","title":"Working with Variables","topics":["Variables","Calculations","Aggregation","Dimensions","Units","TimeSegments","contextVarianceMap","groupSummary","DimensionReference","get-values","compare-cases","valueSummary"]} [ASSISTANT]: Now I have everything I need. The cleanest approach is to gate **New Subscribers** through the Is Live flag, and then the rest cascades naturally (no new subs → no subscriber growth → no revenue pre-launch). ---
### 🚀 Flexible Launch Date
Two new Variables gate the entire model on a configurable launch date:
| Variable | Type | Formula / Value | |----------|------|-----------------| | **Launch Date** | Assumption (Date, constant) | 31 Mar 2026 | | **Is Live** | Calculation (Boolean) | `Periods >= Launch Date` |
The **Subscribers** formula is now: `Subscribers[-1] + New Subscribers * Is Live - Churned Subscribers`
This means New Subscribers only flow in once the model is live — everything cascades from there.
### Updated Values
| Period | Is Live | New Subs | Subscribers | Churned | Revenue | |--------|---------|----------|-------------|---------|---------| | Mar 25 | ❌ | 100 | 0 | 0 | 0 | | Jun 25 | ❌ | 120 | 0 | 0 | 0 | | Sept 25 | ❌ | 140 | 0 | 0 | 0 | | Dec 25 | ❌ | 160 | 0 | 0 | 0 | | **Mar 26** | ✅ | 180 | **180** | 0 | **9,000** | | Jun 26 | ✅ | 200 | 371 | 9 | 18,550 |
Everything zeros out before launch, then ramps up from Q1 2026. Just change the **Launch Date** assumption to shift the entire model's start point! [USER]: And a simple dashboard [ASSISTANT]:

[TOOL]: GetInstructions(action="get", explanation="Get dashboard build instructions", parameters={"category":"build-visualisations"}) [TOOL_RESULT]: {"category":"build-visualisations","content":"# Build Visualisations\n\n## Purpose\nUse this workflow when creating Dashboards, Charts, Metrics, or Tables.\n\n## Prerequisites\n- Model context is set\n- Model structure and calculations exist\n- Retrieve `dashboards` object instructions for API details if you haven't already\n\n## Workflow\n\n### 1. Determine What to Visualise\n- **KPI values** → Create Metrics + add to Dashboard\n- **Trends over time** → Create Charts\n- **Scenario comparison** → Create Tables\n- **Editable inputs** → Create AssumptionsTables\n\n### 2. Create Metrics (if needed)\nMetrics produce single-value outputs from Variables. Required for:\n- Dashboard KPI displays\n- Table inputs (data table comparison)\n\nKey considerations:\n- Must force a single time value (specify a period or aggregate: SUM, MAX, etc.)\n- Can be Dimensional (only time is restricted)\n- Get `dashboards` object instructions for Metric calculation patterns\n\n### 3. Create Dashboard\nA Dashboard is a container for DashboardItems:\n- **Panel**: Container for grouping items\n- **Chart**: Single Variable visualisation\n- **Metric**: Single value display\n- **Table**: Multi-Case comparison (Excel data table)\n- **AssumptionsTable**: Editable assumption inputs\n- **Spacer**: Visual spacing\n\n### 4. Configure Charts\nFor each Chart:\n- Select the Variable to chart\n- Choose 2 of {Time, Dimensions, Scenarios, Sensitivities} for Categories and Series\n- Configure filter values for remaining axes (not interactive — set specific values)\n- Select chart type (Column, Line, Stacked Column, etc.)\n\n### 5. Create Tables (for Scenario Comparison)\nTables compare Metric values across Cases:\n1. Create the Metrics you want to compare\n2. Create a Table referencing those Metrics\n3. Add the Table to a Dashboard\n4. Create a Dashboard ModelSheet for that Dashboard\n\n### 6. Add to Layout\nFor Excel output, Dashboards need a Dashboard ModelSheet:\n- Create a ModelSheet with type `Dashboard`\n- Reference the Dashboard ID\n- **Layout constraint:** If Dashboard has an AssumptionsTable, those Assumptions can't have entry cells elsewhere in the Layout\n\n### 7. Validate\n- Run `ModelAction(action='validate-layout')` to check for Layout conflicts\n- Review Dashboard in app for visual correctness\n","description":"Workflow for creating Dashboards, Charts, Metrics, and Tables","title":"Build Visualisations","topics":["Dashboard","Chart","Metric","Table","visualisation"]} [TOOL]: GetInstructions(action="get", explanation="Get dashboard object instructions", parameters={"category":"dashboards"}) [TOOL_RESULT]: {"category":"dashboards","content":"# Working with Dashboards\r\n\r\n## Overview\r\n\r\nDashboards provide in-app and Excel visualization. A Dashboard contains DashboardItems arranged in a layout.\r\n\r\n## DashboardItem Types\r\n\r\n| Type | Purpose |\r\n|---|---|\r\n| **Panel** | Container for grouping items (has child items) |\r\n| **Chart** | Single Variable visualisation |\r\n| **Metric** | Single value KPI display |\r\n| **Table** | Multi-Case comparison (Excel data table) |\r\n| **AssumptionsTable** | Editable assumption inputs |\r\n| **Spacer** | Visual spacing |\r\n\r\n## Charts\r\n\r\n### One Variable Per Chart\r\nEach Chart displays a single Variable. For multi-Variable comparison, create separate Charts.\r\n\r\n### Axis Configuration\r\nChoose any 2 of the following for **Categories** (x-axis) and **Series** (legend/color):\r\n- **Time**: The Model's time periods\r\n- **Each Dimension**: If the Variable has tagParents (e.g., Region, Product)\r\n- **Scenarios**: Compare across Scenarios\r\n- **Sensitivities**: Compare across Sensitivities\r\n\r\nThe remaining axes become **filters** — configure specific values during chart setup. Filters are NOT interactive in the rendered Dashboard; you select the specific filter values when creating the chart.\r\n\r\n### Chart Types\r\nColumn, Stacked Column, Line, Area, Bar, etc.\r\n\r\n### Example: Revenue by Region and Tier\r\nVariable \"Revenue\" has Dimensions: Region, Tier\r\n\r\n```\r\nCategories: Tier (x-axis shows: Basic, Premium)\r\nSeries: Region (colors show: UK, France)\r\nFilters: Time = \"2025\" (fixed to specific period)\r\n```\r\n\r\nThis creates a stacked column chart showing revenue breakdown by tier and region for 2025.\r\n\r\n## Metrics\r\n\r\n### Single-Value Outputs\r\nMetrics produce a single value from a Variable. You MUST force a single time value:\r\n\r\n**Option A: Specific period**\r\nReference a specific period in the Metric calculation.\r\n\r\n**Option B: Aggregate operation**\r\nUse SUM, MAX, MIN, AVERAGE etc. across the time range.\r\n\r\n### Dimensional Metrics\r\nMetrics CAN be dimensional (the time dimension is the only one restricted). A dimensional Metric will show values for each dimension member.\r\n\r\n### Common Patterns\r\n- \"Total 5-year Revenue\" → SUM aggregate across all periods\r\n- \"Exit Year EBITDA Margin %\" → Reference last period\r\n- \"NPV\" → Calculation using discount rate across periods\r\n\r\n### Retrieving Metric Values\r\n\r\nUse `get-values` to retrieve calculated Metric values:\r\n\r\n```\r\nQueryObject(object_type='Metric', action='get-values', parameters={\r\n 'ids': ['total-revenue', 'exit-margin']\r\n})\r\n```\r\n\r\nCompare across Cases (two modes):\r\n```\r\n# Multi-case mode: tabular view of values across cases (no variance)\r\nQueryObject(object_type='Metric', action='compare-cases', parameters={\r\n 'ids': ['total-revenue'],\r\n 'cases': ['Base/Base', 'Lean/Base', 'Base/High']\r\n})\r\n\r\n# Pairwise mode: detailed variance between two cases\r\nQueryObject(object_type='Metric', action='compare-cases', parameters={\r\n 'ids': ['total-revenue'],\r\n 'baseCase': 'Base/Base',\r\n 'compareCase': 'Lean/Base'\r\n})\r\n```\r\n\r\nAdd `valueSummary` to filter results for quick value context:\r\n```\r\nQueryObject(object_type='Metric', action='filter', parameters={\r\n 'properties': ['name', 'valueSummary']\r\n})\r\n```\r\n\r\n## Tables (Data Tables)\r\n\r\nTables compare Metric values across multiple Cases (Scenario + Sensitivity combinations).\r\n\r\n### Required Workflow\r\n1. **Create Metrics** that produce the values to compare\r\n2. **Create a Table** referencing those Metric IDs\r\n3. **Add the Table to a Dashboard** as a DashboardItem\r\n4. **Create a Dashboard ModelSheet** referencing that Dashboard\r\n\r\nThe Table generates an Excel data table that calculates each Metric for every specified Case combination.\r\n\r\n## AssumptionsTables\r\n\r\nDisplay editable Assumptions within a Dashboard. Key constraint:\r\n- Each Assumption can only have ONE entry cell in Excel\r\n- If a Dashboard with an AssumptionsTable is in the Layout, those Assumptions' entry cells move to the Dashboard sheet\r\n- The original Assumptions sheet will point to the Dashboard\r\n\r\n## Adding Dashboards to Layout\r\n\r\nFor Excel output, create a Dashboard ModelSheet:\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"action\": \"create\",\r\n \"object_type\": \"ModelSheet\",\r\n \"parameters\": {\r\n \"name\": \"Dashboard\",\r\n \"modelSheetType\": \"Dashboard\",\r\n \"dashboardId\": \"-dashboardId\"\r\n }\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\n## Best Practices\r\n\r\n1. Put Tables in their own Dashboard (one Table per Dashboard)\r\n2. Group related Charts and Metrics in Panels\r\n3. Configure chart filters to show the most relevant default view\r\n4. Always validate layout after adding Dashboard sheets\r\n5. Remember: chart filters are set during configuration, not interactive in the dashboard\r\n","description":"Object reference: DashboardItem properties, Chart axis config, Metric period handling, Table workflow, retrieving Metric values","title":"Working with Dashboards","topics":["Dashboard","Chart","Metric","Table","AssumptionsTable","DashboardItem","get-values","compare-cases"]} [ASSISTANT]: Now let me check the schemas for creating Dashboards, DashboardItems, and Metrics:

[TOOL]: GetInstructions(action="get", explanation="Get layout build instructions", parameters={"category":"build-layout"}) [TOOL_RESULT]: {"category":"build-layout","content":"# Build Model Layout\n\n## Purpose\nUse this workflow when creating or modifying the Model's Excel Layout (ModelSheets, LineGroups, LineItems).\n\n## Prerequisites\n- Model context is set\n- Model structure exists (Variables and Assumptions created)\n- Retrieve `layout` object instructions for API details if you haven't already\n\n## Approach: Auto Layout First\n\n### Option A: Automatic Generation (Try First)\n\nThis is the quickest way to create a complete Layout. Auto-layout generates all required sheets (Assumptions and Calculations), traces all dependencies, and places everything correctly.\n\n1. **Identify root Variables** from the Model summary (select all root Variables for a full Model layout)\n2. **Run `create-auto-layout`:**\n ```\n ModelAction(action='create-auto-layout', parameters={\n variableIds: [root Variable IDs]\n })\n ```\n This automatically creates Assumptions and Calculations sheets with all dependencies included. Optional parameters: `calculationsSheetId` (to add to an existing sheet), `maxLevels`, `idealMinGroupsAtLevel`, `idealMaxGroupsAtLevel`.\n3. **Validate:** `ModelAction(action='validate-layout')`\n4. **Review and optionally refine** using `get-content` on each sheet:\n - Flatten overly-nested structures for simple models\n - Apply formatting to LineItems (auto-layout doesn't format)\n - Verify FAST layout principles (dependencies visible above dependents)\n\n### Option B: Manual Layout\n\nUse when auto-layout isn't suitable (specific formatting, custom sheet structure, partial layout updates).\n\n1. **Trace dependencies** on all root Variables to see the full hierarchy\n2. **Plan sheet structure:**\n - One Assumptions sheet (usually sufficient)\n - One or more Calculations sheets (by business area for large models)\n - Metrics sheets if needed\n - Dashboard sheets for visualization\n3. **Create sheets, groups, and items** following the hierarchy\n4. **Validate immediately** after creation\n\n## FAST Layout Principles\n\n- **Repeat Variables freely** — include the same Variable on multiple sheets as needed. Each copy links back to the original. Dependencies should be visible directly above their dependents\n- **Top-to-bottom flow** — inputs at top, outputs at bottom within each sheet\n- **7-10 groups per level** — use nested LineGroups for readability\n- **One timeline per sheet** — don't mix Variables of different `periodType`\n\n## Validation\n\nAlways run `ModelAction(action='validate-layout')` after layout changes. Common issues:\n- Missing Variables (calculation dependencies not in any sheet)\n- Assumption Variables on wrong sheet type\n- Multiple entry cells for the same Assumption (when Dashboards are involved)\n","description":"Workflow for creating ModelSheets, LineGroups, and LineItems","title":"Build Model Layout","topics":["ModelSheet","LineGroup","LineItem","Layout","Excel"]} [ASSISTANT]: Auto-layout looks like the way to go. [TOOL]: GetInstructions(action="get", explanation="Get layout object instructions", parameters={"category":"layout"}) [TOOL_RESULT]: {"category":"layout","content":"# Working with Layout Objects\n\n## Overview\n\nThe Layout contains ModelSheets which define the Excel workbook structure. The Layout object always exists on a Model (cannot be created or deleted).\n\n## ModelSheet Types\n\n| Type | Contains | LineItem Links To |\n|---|---|---|\n| **Calculations** | Variable LineItems | Variables (ALL types, including Assumption-type) |\n| **Assumptions** | ModelAssumption LineItems | ModelAssumptions |\n| **Metrics** | Metric LineItems | Metrics |\n| **Dashboard** | Dashboard reference | N/A |\n| **XlInsert** | External Excel import | N/A (not yet enabled) |\n\n### CRITICAL: Assumption-type Variables go on Calculation sheets\nThis is the most common mistake. An Assumption-type Variable is still a Variable — it goes on a Calculations sheet. Only ModelAssumption objects go on Assumptions sheets. The Assumption-type Variable on the Calculations sheet links to the ModelAssumption on the Assumptions sheet.\n\n## Creating ModelSheets\n\nUse `get-help` for the full schema. Key properties:\n```json\n{\n \"action\": \"create\",\n \"object_type\": \"ModelSheet\",\n \"parameters\": {\n \"name\": \"Income Statement\",\n \"modelSheetType\": \"Calculations\",\n \"contentSections\": [{\n \"lineGroups\": [...]\n }]\n }\n}\n```\n\n## LineGroups\n\nPurely organizational containers with headers. Can be nested.\n\nKey properties:\n- `name`: Group header text\n- `lineItems`: Array of LineItems directly under this group\n- `lineGroups`: Nested LineGroups\n\n**Note:** LineItems within a LineGroup appear after the header and BEFORE any nested LineGroups. Avoid mixing LineItems and nested LineGroups at the same level.\n\n## LineItems\n\nIndividual rows linked to a Variable, ModelAssumption, or Metric.\n\nKey properties:\n- `variableId` / `modelAssumptionId` / `metricId`: The linked object\n- Formatting properties (bold, italic, borders, etc.)\n\n**Dimensional expansion:** A single LineItem for a Dimensional Variable/Metric automatically expands into multiple rows — one per dimension combination. No need to create separate LineItems per dimension member.\n\n## Layout Constraints\n\n- **One timeline per sheet**: Variables with different `periodType` cannot share a sheet\n- **One entry cell per Assumption**: If an Assumption appears in a Dashboard's AssumptionsTable in the Layout, it can't have a separate entry cell on an Assumptions sheet\n- **Minimum for valid Excel**: At least 1 Assumptions sheet + 1 Calculations sheet\n- **Control sheet**: One sheet should have `isControlSheet: true` for the Scenario/Sensitivity dropdown\n\n## Repeated LineItems (FAST Practice)\n\nVariables can appear on multiple sheets. Each additional LineItem links back to the original. This is encouraged — dependencies should be visible directly above their dependents on each sheet.\n\n## Best Practices\n\n1. **Top-to-bottom flow**: Inputs at top, outputs at bottom within each sheet\n2. **7-10 groups per level**: Use nested LineGroups for readability\n3. **Single Assumptions sheet** usually sufficient\n4. **Multiple Calculation sheets** for large models — group by business area\n5. **Always validate** after layout changes: `ModelAction(action='validate-layout')`\n","description":"Object reference: ModelSheet, LineGroup, LineItem properties and constraints","title":"Working with Layout Objects","topics":["ModelSheet","LineGroup","LineItem","Layout","formatting"]} [TOOL]: QueryObject(action="create", explanation="Get LineGroup schema", object_type="LineGroup", parameters={"help":true}) [TOOL]: QueryObject(action="create", explanation="Get LineItem schema", object_type="LineItem", parameters={"help":true}) [TOOL_RESULT]: {"action":"create","description":"Create/Update a new LineGroup. NOTE: same schema for Create/Update actions, don't retrieve both.","notes":["Updates support partial payloads - only include properties you want to change"],"object_type":"LineGroup","parameters":{"includeHeader":{"type":"boolean"},"lineGroups":{"description":"Nested sub-groups. Not needed when creating within ModelSheet content parameter or updating existing LineGroups.","type":"string"},"position":{"description":"Zero-based index position within parent's lineGroups array.
